claim: 93% of lost dogs are found alive, with 20% returning home on their own; microchipped dogs are returned at 52.2% rate vs 21.9% for non-microchipped (AVMA study data).
stance_target: Lost dog recovery is achievable in the majority of cases; identification technology significantly improves outcomes
scope_conditions: US, dogs, data from Lost Pet Research & Recovery and AVMA
quoted_support: Lost Pet Research & Recovery indicates that 93% of lost dogs are found alive, and 20% of them return home on their own. Dogs without microchips were returned to their families 21.9% of the time. In contrast, microchipped dogs were returned 52.2% of the time, even when they were 600 to 1,000 miles away from home.

claim: The median distance lost outdoor-access cats traveled was approximately 344 yards (17-house radius) from home, per MARN missing cat study — a tight spatial prior for feline search concentration.
stance_target: Tight spatial priors exist for lost cat position based on empirical data from the MARN missing cat study
scope_conditions: Outdoor-access cats, MARN missing cat study data
quoted_support: A missing cat study from the Missing Animal Response Network shows that the median distance lost outdoor-access cats traveled was roughly 344 yards, or about a 17-house radius from their home.

claim: One in three pets will become lost in their lifetime, and of 3.3 million dogs and cats entering shelters as strays, only about 20% are reunited with their families.
stance_target: Lost pet recovery is a high-volume problem with currently low systematic success rates
quoted_support: Statistics show that one in three pets will become lost in their lifetime... of 3.3 million dogs and cats who enter shelters as strays, only about 20% are reunited with their families.
